title: Describing a Triangle Function with the Unit Step
summary: This page presents Example 1.6, which demonstrates how to describe a piecewise triangular signal using unit step functions. The signal is decomposed into two components: x1(t), formed by multiplying a ramp by a gate pulse, and x2(t), formed by multiplying a negative-slope ramp by another gate pulse. The final expressions use combinations of unit step functions to represent each interval of the signal.

figures.caption: Representation of a signal defined interval by interval, showing the decomposition of a triangular signal x(t) into two ramp-based components x₁(t) and x₂(t) using unit step functions.
figures.description: The figure has time t on the horizontal axis and signal amplitude on the vertical axis, with labeled plots for x(t), x1(t), and x2(t). Panel (a) shows a triangular signal x(t) that rises linearly from (0,0) to (2,2) and then falls linearly to (3,0), with dashed guides marking the peak value 2 at t=2. Panels (b) and (c) show the component ramps: x1(t) is an increasing segment active from t=0 to t=2, and x2(t) is a decreasing segment active from t=2 to t=3. Students should notice that the original triangle is represented interval by interval as the sum of two ramp-based pieces gated by unit step functions.
